text:
Moderate flare: 30–50;

Severe flare: >50
Ulcerative colitis
In UC, the topography of the lesions differs from that of adults: isolated proctitis is less common and pancolonic involvement is more frequent.
Table 5: Paris classification for pediatric ulcerative colitis (43)
Extension
E1:	proctitis
E2:	left-sided colitis (distal to the splenic flexure)
E3:	extensive colitis (proximal to the splenic flexure)
E4:	pancolitis (proximal to the hepatic flexure)
Severity
S0:		never severe
S1:		at least one severe flare (PUCAI > 65)
The PUCAI activity score (Pediatric Ulcerative Colitis Activity Index) is used to assess disease activity at diagnosis and during follow-up (figure 3).
In pediatric UC, several prognostic factors have been identified by an expert consensus (45): the risk of colectomy is higher in the presence of a family history of UC, extensive disease at diagnosis, or progression of lesions over time. Conversely, the presence of primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) appears to be associated with a lower rate of surgery.
